theory.heading: Present Simple
theory.subheading:
Affirmative

I, you, we, they swim

He, she, it swims

With most verbs, we add an -s to the third person singular but only in the affirmative form. This is shown in the example above

When verbs end in o, s, sh, ch or x we istend add an -es to the third person singular, for example, I watch TV becomes She watches TV, or I wash my car becomes He washes his car.

When verbs end in a consonant + -y, such as the verbs try or apply, we replace the -y with -ies so that I try becomes She tries, and I apply becomes It applies

 

Negative

I, you, we, they don't swim / do not swim

He, she, it is doesn't swim / does not swim

 

Interrogative

Do I, you, we, they swim?

Does he, she, it swim?

 

Short anwers

Yes, I do.

No, you don't

Yes, he does

No, she doesn't

We use the present simple to talk about:

  1. Daily routines or habits. Every day I get up at 7.30.
  2. Timetables. The shops in this country open at 5 o'clock
  3. General truths. The Sun sets in the East.
  4. Stative verbs. I know the truth now.
  5. Future reference. When I see you tomorrow, I'll tell you the truth.

Stative verbs include most verbs related to emotions, existence, senses, likes and dislikes, beliefs and possessions

Some examples of stative verbs include see, taste, hear, hate, like, prefer, fancy, want, love, wish, believe, know, understand, realise, appear, seem, look like, own, belong, have (for possession), smell and taste

The present simple tense can be used with a wide variety of expressions related to time and frequency. Here are some of the more common choices:

Always, usually, often, sometimes, hardly ever, seldom, never Every day/week/month/year, in the morning/afternoon/evening Once, twice, three times, four times, etc

Here are a few examples of these expressions being used with the present simple tense:

  • I'm always late for school
  • I catch the train every day
  • I usually drink a cup of coffee in the morning
  • I go to the gym three times a week but John never goes

theory.heading: Present Continuous
theory.subheading:
We form the present continuous using the present tense of the verb be and the -ing form of a verb

Affirmative

I am swimming
You, we, they are swimming
He, she, it is swimming
 

Negative Form

We add 'not' after the verb 'be' and before the -ing verb. We can also make use of contractions, such as contracting I aminto I’m or It isinto It’s

I am not swimming
You, we, they are not swimming
He, she, it is not swimming
I'm not swimming
You, we, they aren't swimming
He, she, it isn't swimming

 

Interrogative

In order to make the interrogative form, we put the verb in front of the subject.

Am I swimming
Are you, we, they swimming
Is he, she swimming
 

Short Anwers

Yes, I am - No, I am not - Yes, he is.
No, he isn't - Yes, they are - No, they aren't.
'

theory.title: Spelling Rules
theory.subheading:
1. Verbs ending in -e drop the -e when adding -ing
Write > Writing
2. Verbs ending in -l drop the -l when adding -ing
Tavel > Travelling

3. Verbs that are formed by one or two consonants, plus a vowel, plus another consonant,sit or trip, for example, double the last consonant when we add -ing

Sit > Sitting 
Trip > Tripping
Run > Running
Stop > Stopping

 

Use

1. We use the present continuous to talk about actions that we are doing
     Right now, I am writing.
     I am stuying English.
 
2. We also use it to refer to future arrangements with other people. These arrangements must be plans that are fixed at a particular time ad are usually specified with time reference words, like tonight, tomorrow, on Monday, next week/month/year or the following night/day
 
     I am meeting her tonight.
     I am going to France next week.
     I am going to the concert on Friday and visiting the museum the following day.
 
3. We can also use this tense to talk about temporary situations
    Marc is at university. He is studying maths.
    John is unemployed. He is looking for a job.
    He is staying in a hotel.

'

theory.title: A day in a life of a teenager
theory.heading: In the morning
theory.subheading:
Hello there,
Let me introduce myself. My name is Raphael and I live in Durham, in the north-east of England.
On weekdays, I get up at 7:30am and make my bed quickly. After getting dressed, I go downstairs and prepare my breakfast. I like to have a bowl of cereal, toast with butter and a mug of hot milk. Mind you, when it starts getting warmer I switch to cold milk or a milkshake.
You know what? I have a pet canary. My mum has taught me to clean out its cage every morning. I need to get the dirt out. Also, I have to clean up the mess outside. Like most birds, he throws the husks out of his cage.
Husks must be cleaned, otherwise Mum gets upset.
I say goodbye to Bartol, my canary, and I grab my backpack. After checking everything I need for the day is inside, I head to school. I needn’t take the bus since my home is just round the corner from the school. I’m really lucky that way. If I lived a long way from school, I´d have to wake up much earlier, which I am not too fond of!
School is hard work but I’m proud of being part of such an excellent institution. It was awarded a prize last year for being rated ‘excellent’ and top of the list of schools in the area in terms of quality and students’ grades.
At school, I have a break at 10.30am. I go out into the playground and have some fun with my mates. We like to play either marbles or catch. The day goes quickly and before we know it we are packing up our books and tidying our desks at 3.00pm, ready to go home.

theory.title: A day in a life of a teenager
theory.heading: In the afternoon and evening
theory.subheading:
Dinner is usually waiting for me at home. I also help Mum with the housework. My job is to empty the dishwasher and put the plates away.
After that, I do my homework. My best friend Tom usually struggles with maths and science in class, so he needs to have a private tutor. This means that he doesn’t go out on Mondays and Wednesdays after school. However, on the other weekdays we meet up and play football with a local club.
We are really into football. Our coach has always talked us into doing our best. Tom is a goalkeeper and I play midfield. We want to be professional football players. Time will tell, I guess.
I’m back home by around 8p.m. I have a shower and put on my pyjamas. Before going to bed I play computer games for an hour. I set my alarm clock, get into bed and usually sleep like a log.

theory.heading: Comparative and Superlative Adjectives and Adverbs
theory.subheading:
We use comparatives and superlatives to compare things. We can use them to explain that a noun (a place, person or thing) has more, less or the same amount of a particular quality than one or more other nouns. For example, ‘this tree is taller than that tree’ or ‘she is more intelligent than her sister’.

 

In order to form comparatives (when they are not equal) and superlatives, we need to divide adjectives and adverbs into two groups.




Group 1:.

One-syllable adjectives/adverbs and those with a ‘-y’ ending, such as ‘tall’, ‘short’ or ‘pretty’


Group 2:

More than one syllable adjectives and adverbs, and those ending in ‘-ed’ or ‘-ing’, such as ‘intelligent’, ‘bored’ or ‘annoying’.




COMPARATIVES

 

-Group 1 forms the comparative by adding ‘-er' to the adjective, for example:

  •      Tall ---------- Taller
  •      Pretty ---------- Prettier
  •      Late ---------- Later

 

-Group 2 allows the adjective to stay the same but puts ‘more’ in front of it.

  • Intelligent ---------- More intelligent  
  • Bored ---------- More bored

 

Both of these groups of comparatives are usually followed by ‘than’ when the second part of the comparison is mentioned (the noun that we are comparing our first noun to). For example:

 

Alan is older than Paul.

Your cat is more affectionate than mine.

You can talk louder than me.

Equality

We use as + adjective or adverb + as to say when two things or people are equal. There is no distinction between long and short adjectives/adverbs in this case.

Ann is as late as Tom in the mornings.

Peter’s house is as modern as Elsie’s house.

 

Alternatively 'so' can be used instead of the first 'as'when the sentence is negative.

You are not so stubborn as your sister.

Inequality: Less


Sometimes we want to pay attention to something or someone that belongs to a lower scale. In this case we use:

 

Comparative: Less + adj/adv + than
Superlative: The least + adj/adv
 
For example:

 Alex is less cheerful than his brother.

Tom is the least tall in the classroom.

'

theory.title: As much as & As many as
theory.subheading:
When we use nouns we can also make comparisons by using ‘as much as’, with uncountable nouns, and ‘as many as’, with countable nouns.

He has as much money as me.

There are as many chairs as there are desks.

In fact, we can even use ‘as much as’ or ‘as many as’ on their own, without a noun. When we do this it is still clear that we are referring to quantity.

I know as much as you.

 

The more, The Better (or 'the... the...')

To say that things or people change at the same time we use ‘the’ + comparative and ‘the’ + comparative again. This is used to show that a particular increase or decrease is proportionate.

For example, if we want to ask our teacher how much English practice we should do per week they might reply ‘the more, the better’, meaning that the more practice we do, the better it is.

Or, if we are introduced to someone and initially like them but discover, after meeting them a third or fourth time, that we don’t like this person as much as we thought we did, we might say ‘the more I see her, the less I like her’.

'

theory.heading: Past Simple
theory.subheading:
We form the past simple tense by adding ‘-ed’ to regular verbs.

I, you, he, she, it, we, you, they worked (work).

When the verb is irregular, each form has an irregular form and has to be learnt.

I, you, he, she, it, we, you, they ate (eat).

Look at the list of irregular verbs at the end of the book.

-ed Spelling rules

  • Verbs that end in ‘-e’ only add ‘-d’ to form the past: I danced (dance).
  • One-syllable verbs with a single vowel, followed by a consonant, double the final consonant when adding ‘-ed’: I robbed (rob).
  • When the verb ends in consonant + y, we change the ‘y’ to ‘i’ and add ‘-ed’: I studied (study).
  • When a verb ends in vowel + y, we add ‘-ed’: I played (play).
  • When the stress is on the final syllable of a verb that ends in vowel + consonant, we double the consonant: I preferred (prefer).
  • If the verb ends in ‘-el’, we double the ‘l’ when adding ‘-ed’ (mainly in British English): I travelled (travel).

Pronunciation of '-ed'

‘-ed’ verbs can be pronounced in three different ways:

  1. /id/ When the last sound of the word, without the ‘-ed’ ending, is a ‘t’ or a ‘d’. For example: needed, wanted and decided.
  2. /t/ Verbs that end in voiceless sounds, like ‘p’, ‘k’, ’f’, ‘s’, ‘ch’, ‘sh’, ‘x’ and ‘th’. Examples of these are washed, looked and stopped.
  3. /d/ Verbs that end in ‘b’, ‘g’, ‘j’, ‘m’, ‘n’, ‘l’, ‘r’, ‘th’, ‘w’, ‘v’, ‘z’. Examples include saved, cried and allowed.

Negative Form

I, you, he, she, it, we, you, they + didn’t/did not + infinitive form

I didn't work
I didn't eat

Interrogative Form

Did + I, you, he, she, it, we, you, they + infinitive form

Did I work?
I didn't work

Short answers

Did you work?
Yes, I did. / No I didn’t.

theory.heading: Past Simple
theory.subheading:
We use past simple for:

1. Actions that are perceived as finished at a specific time in the past.

I went to London last year.
He got up at 7:30a.m. yesterday

2. Past habits.

When I was a child, I often enjoyed cartoons on TV.

3. Series of completed actions that happened one after the other in the past.

First I switched on the lights, then I got up and got dressed.

4. An action that interrupted another action that was in progress in the past.

While I was reading, the telephone rang.

Time expressions used in the past simple:

  • Yesterday
  • Last night/week/month/year
  • In 2008
  • When I was a kid
  • Two days/weeks/months/years... ago
  • Yesterday morning/afternoon

theory.heading: Past Continuous
theory.subheading:
Affirmative Form

We form the past continuous with the past simple of ‘be’ (was, were) + a verb -ing.

I, he, she, it was playing.

You, we, they were playing.

Negative

We just use the negative form of the verb ‘be’ in the past.

I, he, she, it wasn’t / was not playing.

You, we, they weren’t / were not playing.

Interrogative

Was I, he, she, it playing?

Were you, we, they playing?

Short answers

Was I studying? Yes, I was. / No, I wasn’t.

Were they working? Yes, they were. / No, they weren’t.

1. Actions that we perceive as in progress at a specific time in the past (we look at these actions as if they were not finished at the time we are referring to).

I remember that evening you were visiting your Grandma.

2. Actions that were in progress when another action interrupted them.

I was watching TV when Ann came into the living-room.

3. Two or more actions that were happening at the same time.

While I was cooking, my sister was washing the dishes.

4. We tend to use past continuous for long actions and past simple for shorter actions.

I was sitting there all day.
I sat there for fifteen minutes.

The following time expressions are commonly used with past continuous:

while
when
as
all day/night

theory.heading: Too, enough, too much and too many
theory.subheading:
'Too' and ‘enough’ indicate degree. They can modify adjectives, adverbs and nouns.

  • Too means ‘more than what is needed’.
  • Enough means ‘sufficient’.

‘Too’ has a negative meaning. It comes before adjectives or adverbs.

He is too old to play with the children.

‘Enough’ comes after adjectives or adverbs.

Tom is not old enough to drive a car.

Too + nouns

When we use nouns with ‘too’ we must add an extra word.

We say ‘Too much’ for uncountable nouns.
We say ‘Too many’ for countable nouns.

There is too much salt in this salad.

There are too many children in this classroom.

Enough + nouns

When ‘enough’ collocates with nouns, it must be placed before them.

I haven’t got enough money to buy a new laptop.

theory.heading: Present Perfect
theory.subheading:
Affirmative Form

I, you, we, they have + past participle (‘-ed’ or the third column of the irregular verbs list)

He, she, it has + past participle

I have walked.

He has read.

Contracted forms:

I’ve walked
He’s read.

Negative Form

Have not or haven’t + p.p.

They haven´t walked.

Has not or hasn’t + p.p.

He hasn’t read.

Interrogative Form

Have I, you, we, they + p.p.?

Have you studied?

Has he, she, it + p.p.?

Has he left?

Short answers

Have you studied?
Yes, I have.
Has he left?
No, he hasn’t.

Use

Present perfect is used when there is a connection between the present and the past.

1. Actions which start in the past and continues up to the present.

I have lived in this town for 30 years.

2. Recently completed actions.

I have just washed my hair. It is still wet.

3. Experiences or changes in our lives.

I have never been to London.

This is the funniest film I have ever seen.

theory.heading: Been and Gone
theory.subheading:
We use the present perfect of ‘be’ when someone has gone to a place and returned.

I have been to Ireland.

However, when someone has not returned, we use ‘gone’.

She's gone to Paris for a week. She’ll be back tomorrow.

Time Expressions

Since: It refers to the period of time between something starting and the present. The action in question must have continued without interruption between these two moments.

I have been in this park since 7pm.

I haven’t seen her since high school.

For: It implies a total duration, a complete period of time.

He’s played tennis for 3 years.

I’m going to Rome for 10 days.

Already: It means that something is done. It can take a middle position or the final position in a clause.

I’ve already finished my homework.

I’ve finished my homework already.

Yet: It’s used when something is not done but implies that it will be done in the future. It takes the final position in a clause.

Have you done the washing up yet?

I haven´t seen him yet.

Just: We use it when something was done very recently, usually a few minutes ago. It goes after the auxiliary verb and before the main verb.

He’s just picked up his children and is coming back home.

No, thanks. I have just eaten.

Still: It means that something is not done yet but implies that this action is some kind of goal or obligation. It is placed before the negative auxiliary verb.

I still haven’t found what I’m looking for.

He still hasn’t paid me back the money he owes me.

Ever: Used to show that we are talking about an action happening at any point, any time, perhaps in our entire life or perhaps in the entire history of the universe. It goes between the auxiliary verb and the main verb.

Have you ever been to Madrid?

This is the best meat I have ever eaten.

He is the best football player ever.

Some other words that are common with the present perfect include: Recently, lately, so far, until now, up to now.

theory.heading: Countable and Uncountable nouns
theory.subheading:
It is important to learn about countable and uncountable nouns in English because sometimes they do not fit with your own language.

Uncountable nouns are always referred to as singular in English. They cannot be plural themselves and cannot take the article ‘a’ or ‘an’. We can recognise an uncountable noun when we cannot put a number in front of it.

     Water
     Some water
     A water
     Two waters

A countable noun can be counted, and always has the ability to be made plural. A singular countable noun must have an article in front of it. The article could be ‘a’, ‘an’ or ‘the’. We can put any number we like in front of a countable noun.

     A book
     The book
     Some books
     Three books

Uncountable nouns often refer to:

  • Abstract concepts (love, peace, sadness, justice).
  • Liquids (tea, juice, blood, milk).
  • Food (meat, spinach, rice, bread).
  • Substances (wood, coal, paper, oil).
  • Subjects (maths, chemistry, geography, economics).

And many others (homework, mail, music, advice, luggage, information, etc.).

If you need to count an uncountable noun you can do it by adding partitives or prepositional phrases. These let us count the units, instead of the noun itself.

A piece of information
Two flashes of lightning
A flock of sheep
Three slices of cheese
A school of fish
Four bars of soap
A stroke of luck
Five spoonfuls of sugar

Some nouns can change from countable to uncountable, depending on the situation.

Life is good
(Used in the uncountable sense, indicating the concept of life.)
Many lives were lost.
(Uncountable, indicating the concept of work.)
Work is boring.
(Uncountable, indicating the concept of work.)
The works of Dickens.
(Countable, Charles Dickens wrote many great works.)

As you have probably noticed in your English studies, some words look the same but actually mean different things. In these cases, one of these words is often countable, while the other is uncountable. This can help us to recognize which word is being used from the context.

An iron.
(The household appliance.)
It’s made of iron..
(The metal.)
A paper.
(A short form of the word ‘newspaper’.)
It’s made of paper.
(The substance.)

Additionally, I’d like to draw your attention to some words with an ‘s’ ending which are actually uncountable nouns and, therefore, referred to in the singular, despite looking like they are plural.

Economics, politics, news, linguistics, genetics, physics, mumps, diabetes, measles.

They belong to the field of studies, games and illnesses.

One of the most important things to remember about countable and uncountable nouns is that when we speak about something in general we don’t use an article, and when we speak about something in particular we do.

Did you hear the news?
(In particular.)
I love good news.
(In general.)

However, when we are speaking in general about a countable noun, we also have to remember to make it plural.

I like the dog.
(In particular.)
I like dogs.
(In general.)

theory.heading: Quantifiers
theory.subheading:
Some Plural countable nouns and singular uncountable nouns. Affirmative and interrogative sentences (offers, invitations, requests).
Any Plural countable nouns and singular uncountable nouns. Negative and interrogative sentences. Note: When in the affirmative, 'any' means 'it does not matter which'.
No Singular and plural countable nouns and singular uncountable nouns. Affirmative sentences only (usually used for emphasis).
A lot of, lots of, loads of Plural countable nouns and singular uncountable nouns. Affirmative (preferably).
Many Plural countable nouns. Affirmative, neg, interrogative (a bit more formal than 'a lot of').
Much Singular uncountable nouns. Negative and interrogative (very formal, affirmative).
A good/
great deal of		 Plural countable nouns and singular uncountable nouns. Affirmative (preferably).
Plenty of Plural countable nouns and singular uncountable nouns. Aff., neg., interrogative.
A large amount/
quantity of		 Singular uncountable nouns Aff., neg., interrogative.
A large number of Plural countable nouns. Aff., neg., interrogative.
Few, a few Plural countable nouns. Aff., neg., interrogative.
Little, a little, a bit of Singular uncountable nouns Aff., neg., interrogative.
Several Plural countable nouns. Aff., neg., interrogative.
Both Plural countable nouns. Aff., neg., interrogative.
All Uncountable nouns and plural countable nouns (Occasionally works with singular countable nouns, such as when referencing time). Aff., neg., interrogative.
Every Singular countable nouns. Aff., neg., interrogative.
Each Singular countable nouns. Aff., neg., interrogative.

theory.heading: Examples
theory.subheading:
Some There is some water in the bottle.
There are some children in the field.
Would you like some crisps?
Any There isn't any water in the bottle.
There aren’t any children in the field.
Any colour will be fine.
You can take any book you like.
No I’ve got no money this weekend.
He has no hair.
A lot of, lots of, loads of He has a lot of money.
She has lots of good ideas.
She has loads of friends.
Many Are there many coins in that bag?
I enjoyed many sunny days there.
Much I don’t have much time left.
Why is there so much smoke in your room?
A good/great deal of I have spent a great deal of time in England.
Plenty of We have plenty of food so take as much as you want.
Plenty of shops accept credit cards nowadays.
A large amount/quantity of I got a large amount of feedback from my teacher.
A large number of A large number of emails were sent by the locals.
Few, a few Few people like to be insulted.
Only a few students remained in the classroom.
Little, a little, a bit of Little thought has been given to this issue.
I only have a little chocolate but we can share it.
I got a bit of advice from my friends.
Several Several players were sent off the field..
Both I like both shows: Big Brother and Friends.
All I don´t accept all the invitations I get, only some of them.
I’ve been here all day.
Every Almost every answer was correct.
Each There are television sets in each room of the house.

Additional Information on Quantifiers


The quantifiers all, both, each, little, few, some, any, many, much can also use the pattern ‘of the’ when you are talking about a specific group of things or people.

Few of the students passed the test.

All of the money was stolen from the till.

I bought new t-shirts for many of the players.

I spend much of the time alone.

theory.heading: Past Perfect and -ed/-ing ending adjectives
theory.subheading:
Affirmative

I, you, he, she, it, we, you, they + had + past participle

All forms of the present perfect use the auxiliary verb had.

I had studied.
They had fallen.

 

Negative

Had + not + p.p. or Hadn’t + p.p.

I had not waited.
They hadn't eaten.

 

Interrogative

Had + subject + p.p.

Had you worked?
Had they swum?

 

Short answers

Yes, I had.
No, I hadn't.

 

Use

We use the past perfect tense for actions that happened in the past before another action in the past. We can think of it as being like ‘the past of the past’.

When I got home, James had already left.

In this example, the first event is that James left. Then, after that, I got home.

Using the past perfect helps the listener to understand that, although both of these events took place in the past, they did not happen at the same time.

Remember that we only use the past perfect when we want to refer to Remember that we only use the past perfect when we want to refer to a past that is earlier than another time in the narrative. Remember that we only use the past perfect when we want to refer to

Here are a few time words that are commonly used with the past perfect:

Already, for, since, and yet may be used with the past perfect in exactly the same way as they are used with the present perfect.

Other commonly used time expressions are after, before, by the time and until.

I didn´t say anything until she had finished her homework 

By the time she phoned her, she had found someone new 

NOTE:
In addition to past perfect, sometimes referred to as the past perfect simple, there is also a tense called the past perfect continuous, which tends to make an emphasis on duration. It takes the following form:

Had been + -ing.

I had been waiting for her for half an hour when she arrived.

We had been dating for three years when we got married.

'

theory.title: -ed/-ing ending adjectives
theory.subheading:
Adjectives like ‘boring’ and ‘bored’ can be confusing for English learners.

The ending ‘-ed’ is used when the adjective describes a feeling or emotion.

‘I am bored.’ means ‘I feel boredom.’. The person saying this is experiencing boredom at this moment.

On the contrary adjectives that end in '-ing' refer to the cause of that feeling or emotion.

‘This film is boring.’ means this film is causing boredom.

Here is a list of the most common adjectives ending in -ed and -ing:

theory.heading: ing, to infinitve, bare infinitive verbs
theory.subheading:
-Ing Form

We use -ing forms in the following cases:

1. As the subject of a sentence:

Smoking is not a healthy habit.

2. After prepositions:

Smoking is not a healthy habit.

3. After ‘go’, when referring to activities, hobbies and sports:

I like going shopping.

He goes skiing in winter.

4. When placing a verb after certain other verbs, such as: Like, enjoy, hate, dislike, fancy, finish, mind, avoid, miss, suggest, keep, practise, risk, deny, imagine, etc.

Eve enjoys listening to reggae music.

He denied having stolen the purse.

I like reading before bed.

We finished eating and paid the bill.

5. After certain expressions, such as: Can’t help, can’t stand, look forward to, get/be used to, feel like, be (not) worth, etc.

Elvis can’t help falling in love with you.

I look forward to hearing from you.

You’ll get used to working here.

The musical ‘Hamilton’ isn’t worth seeing.

'

To infinitive 

We use the to infinitive in the following cases:

1. After a number of different adjectives:

Chinese is difficult to learn.

It’s nice to see you.

He isn’t easy to work with.

She’s great to be around.

2. To show purpose:

He went to school to learn Maths and Languages.

3. After the following three verbs: Would like, would love and would prefer.

I´d like to stay in today.

I would prefer to sing rather than dance.

4. When placing a verb after certain other verbs, such as: Want, wish, decide, agree, afford, arrange, choose, hope, learn, plan, promise, manage, refuse, pretend, fail, expect, etc.

He can’t afford to buy a new car.

I plan to ask her on a date.

She hopes to study medicine.

We didn’t expect to meet today.

'

Verbs followed either by -ing or to infinitive forms 

After the following verbs, -ing or ‘to infinitive’ can be used without a difference in meaning:

Begin, start, intend, attempt, hate, prefer, love.

I began studying/to study music last year.

He hates to get up early/getting up early.

As for ‘like’, there is very little difference between the two. We usually use ‘like + to’ when we are talking about habits and ‘like + -ing’ when its meaning is similar to ‘enjoy’.

I like dancing.

I like to have lunch early on Sundays.

This chart shows the verbs with a difference in meaning:

Forget/ RememberWhen the action happens at the same time or later than the main verb:
Don’t forget to lock the door.
Remember to call me tonight.		When the action happened earlier than the main verb:
I’d forgotten telling him.
I remember walking in the park when I was a child.
TryMaking an effort:
I tried to pass the test. 		Doing an experiment:
If you cannot open the jar, try using a knife.
StopWhen indicating purpose:
He stopped to smoke a cigarette. 		When abandoning something, such as a habit:
He stopped smoking at the age of 20.
RegretWhen giving bad news that is not necessarily your fault:
I regret to inform you that the shop is closed. 		If you are sorry about something you did:
I regret telling her the secret yesterday.

'

Bare Infinitive

The infinitive can be used with and without ‘to’. When we don’t use ‘to’, we call it the ‘bare infinitive’.

We use bare infinitives:

1. After most modal verbs:

I can go upstairs.

He mustn’t smoke in here.

2. After ‘had better’ and ‘would rather’:

He’d better stay, as it is very late.

I´d rather talk to you later.

 

NOTE:
‘Make’ and ‘let’ are followed by an object (a pronoun or a noun) + bare infinitive:

He made the child cry.

They let me go out tonight.

'

questions.title: 1. Without a doubt, advertising is all around us. The most popular forms of advertising in the modern world are TV, magazine and internet advertising. Television advertising stands out though. Not everybody reads and not all of us have regular access to the internet, however, almost everybody in the Western world has a television set at home. For this reason, ads on TV have a captive audience and are able to reach a huge number of people, which they do very easily. Who doesn’t have a favourite series on TV which they follow daily or weekly? Who is not hooked on a drama series or a soap opera? Who does not get informed about the world through television news?

answers: A. Television viewers outnumber internet users.

questions.title: 2.This mass media device is the perfect way to enter our living rooms and our minds, and companies that are eager to sell their products or services know this all too well. No wonder it costs thousands of euros to purchase a single minute of air-time.

answers: A. Television viewers outnumber internet users.
C. The most powerful advertising medium.
H. The ball is still in our court.
questions.title: 3. Additionally, there is another highly effective method of advertising: Word of mouth. Expensive overheads are unnecessary in this case, in fact, businesses can dramatically increase their profits in this fashion without spending a single euro. Instead, clients and customers who have had a positive experience with a company may tell their friends and relatives, and do the advertiser's work for them. As a result, the number of customers increases, as do company profits, due simply to excellent customer service and/or high quality goods.

answers: A. Television viewers outnumber internet users.
C. The most powerful advertising medium.
H. The ball is still in our court.
questions.title: 4. Of course, this works both ways. It's frequently said by customer service and marketing departments that a customer who has a positive experience with a company will tell one person about it. Conversely, a person who has a negative experience will tell twelve. Clearly word of mouth advertising is a double-edged sword and is effective only for those companies that provide top quality service, which perhaps makes word of mouth advertising all the more valuable.

answers: A. Television viewers outnumber internet users.
C. The most powerful advertising medium.
H. The ball is still in our court.
questions.title: 5. The benefits that a business can acquire from advertising are obvious, but is the buyer also affected so positively? Not always. Let’s look at the downside of advertising. Since we live in a consumer society we are prone to shopping for too many items, many of them unnecessary. 'Retail therapy' and 'shopaholic' are well-known phrases in today's world and are no longer taken as lightly as they once were. They refer to the excessive shopping that we are persuaded to do by the aforementioned advertisements. Do we really need to have such an enormous amount of possessions at home? And if so, to what extent?

answers: A. Television viewers outnumber internet users.
C. The most powerful advertising medium.
H. The ball is still in our court.
questions.title: 6. Ads are not just a sales technique, they can become works of art and persuasion. Firstly, when they are backed up by huge budgets or talented directors, they can create fascinating and compelling video clips which are, every once in a while, awarded prizes for their originality. Scenery, cast, soundtrack and special effects are all cleverly exploited to get the viewer glued to the television set and, in turn, the product. They don't want the spell holding your attention to be broken by a single overlooked detail. They want to keep you interested in watching to get you interested in buying. Secondly, the concepts are thought up by experienced teams of psychologists who, by knowing how the human mind operates, take full advantage of this knowledge to trigger the ‘desire point’, the ‘I want to be the best’, competitive aspect of our mind, and efficiently manipulate us into buying objects we could often do without.

answers: A. Television viewers outnumber internet users.
C. The most powerful advertising medium.
H. The ball is still in our court.
questions.title: 7. We live in a capitalist world. It seems difficult to resist the temptation of acquiring the latest technological device, pair of shoes or household appliance, in the belief that they will make us feel happier, more beautiful, more intelligent or even reach a higher social status. Appearance is everything, they say. Advertising is likely to be blamed for this erroneous belief. We let advertisers enter our homes and become part of our day-to-day lives and, whether we want them to be or not, they are always there. Consequently, we get immersed in a series of purchases that reduce our income, spending money that could be invested much more effectively elsewhere. Advertising does a great job of obtaining these funds from us.

answers: A. Television viewers outnumber internet users.
C. The most powerful advertising medium.
H. The ball is still in our court.
questions.title: 8. On the other hand, ads bring us up to date. They help us find out about the latest technologies and products that are available to us. If we are in need of updated information on the new advances of a particular type of product then ads may be our ally.   We must be fair and recognise this benefit that we all get from them. We might be clever enough to make use of the information they give us and carefully think it over before deciding whether we need to purchase a product or not. Or, we may act rashly, without thinking, and spend our hard-earned money on things we don't need before realising it's too late. The key is to take our time and think about if we really need a product, or if we are merely being seduced and swept along in the excitement of the moment that the advertisement has created. The latter, of course, is exactly what the advertisers are hoping for.

answers: A. Television viewers outnumber internet users.
C. The most powerful advertising medium.
H. The ball is still in our court.

theory.heading: Such and So
theory.subheading:
Both ‘so’ and ‘such’ have the same meaning, and mean ‘very’, or ‘that much’. We use them to express that something has a high degree of a particular quality.

‘Such’ is a determiner and ‘so’ is an adverb. This means that ‘such’ precedes noun phrases and ‘so’ precedes adjectives and adverbs.

We often use ‘so’ and ‘such’ to add emphasis.

So + adjective/adverbSuch (a) + noun phrase
Tom is so tall.That is such nonsense.
We were so late.Tom is such a tall boy.

We tend to use ‘so’ and ‘such’ before that-clauses, for example:

The skyscraper was so high that I could see the clouds.

It was such a hot day that we all bought cold drinks.

They are such lovely kittens that they sleep in our bed.

‘Such’ can also mean ‘this type of…’, as shown here:

He has never made such mistakes before.

How could you say such a thing?

So + many/much

We can use noun phrases after ‘so much’ and ‘so many’ to show there is a lot of something.

So much + uncountable nounsSo many + countable nouns
We have so much food.She has so many cats.
He has so much money that he could afford a Rolls Royce.I've got so many books that I don’t know where to keep them all.

So + few/little

We can use ‘so few’ and ‘so little’ in a similar way, to show that there is not a lot of something.

So little + uncountable nounsSo few + countable nosuns
We have so little time.There are so few chairs.
They have so little water that they must buy bottles of it every day.Ann has so few friends that she is alone most of the time.

We can also use ‘so much’, ‘so many’, ‘so few’ and ‘so little’ without a noun.

He ate so much that he got sick.

I learnt so little at my old school.

theory.heading: Future Will
theory.subheading:
Affirmative

In this tense, the form is the same for all the people.

I, you, he, she, it, we, you, they will + infinitive verb

I will go.
Contracted form: I´ll go.

Negative

Will + not, or won’t + infinitive verb

I will not go.
I won´t go.
They will not play.
They won’t play.

Interrogative

Will + subject + infinitive verb

Will I go?
Will you play?

Short Answers

Will I go?
Yes, I will./No, I won’t.

Use

1. Decisions made at the moment of speaking, for example:

John: I am really cold.

Sarah: Okay, I will close the window.

2. Predictions which are just based on what one may think, such as:

I think my team will win the cup.

3. Facts about the future, for instance:

Next year, I will be one year older than I am now.

The meeting will take place in August.

4. Also, we often use ‘(don’t) think’, ‘expect’ and ‘be + sure’ before ‘will’, for example:

I don’t think I will go to the party.

I expect James will be there and I don’t like him.

I am sure he will pass the test.

theory.heading: Be going to
theory.subheading:
Affirmative

We form this tense with the verb ‘be’ + ‘going to’ + infinitive.

I am going to play tennis.
He is going to be late.

We can also use the contracted form, for example:

She’s going to move to Dublin.

Negative

To make the negative of this tense, we put the negative on the verb ‘be’.

I am not going to see a movie.
It isn’t going to rain today.

Interrogative

To make the interrogative form, we reverse the order of the verb ‘be’ and the subject.

Are you going to leave now?
Is she going to rent a new flat?

Use

1. To talk about plans and intentions:

I’m going to drive to work today.

2. To make a prediction based on evidence:

That man is driving his car too fast. He’s going to crash.

Look at those dark clouds. I think it’s going to rain.

 

As a Reminder:

- We use the present continuous for arrangements with other people.

I am seeing Tom tonight at 8 pm.

- We use present simple for timetables.

The shops open at 5pm every afternoon in Spain.

- We also use present simple after time words, like ‘when’, ‘as soon as’, ‘after’, ‘before’, ‘until’, etc. when they refer to a future action or state.

Tomorrow, when I get home, I’ll cook my own dinner.

theory.heading: Future Perfect
theory.subheading:
Affirmative

Will have + past participle.

I will have finished by 7.00.        Contracted form: I’ll have finished.

 

Negative

Will not/won’t + have + past participle.

I won’t have finished by 7.00.

 

Interrogative

Will + subject + have + past participle?

Will you have finished by 7.00?

 

Use

We use the Future Perfect to talk about actions that will be finished by a certain time in the future.

 

By the time you get home, I will have arrived in Rome.

 

‘By’ and ‘by the time’ are time particles that we often use with this tense. They both mean ‘not later than a specific moment’. In + time expression is also common.

 

In three years’ time, I will have finished my studies.

theory.heading: Future Continuous
theory.subheading:
Affirmative

Will + be + -ing verb

He will be sunbathing in Málaga in July.

Contracted form: He’ll be sunbathing.

Negative

Will not/won’t + be + -ing verb

Alex won’t be sunbathing in Málaga in July.

Interrogative

Will + subject + be + -ing verb?

Will they be sunbathing in Málaga in July?

Use

We use future continuous to talk about an action that won’t be finished at or around a time in the future, because it will be in progress.

This time next week, I will be lying on the beach.

Time expressions that we often use with Future Continuous include this time and at this time.

theory.heading: Present Perfect Continuous
theory.subheading:
Affirmative

We form the present perfect continuous with:

Have/has + been + -ing verb

I have been working very hard this year.

My phone hasn’t been charging properly.

 

Negative

In the negative we turn ‘have/has’ into the negative forms haven’t and hasn’t.

I haven’t been studying lately.

She hasn’t been thinking clearly recently.

 

Interrogative

In the interrogative form we put the auxiliary verb ‘have/ has’ in front of the subject:

Has she been studying?

Have you been drinking?

 

Use

We use present perfect continuous:

1. In a similar way to present perfect, for actions that started in the past and continue up to the present.

I have been living in my home town since I was born.

2. When we really want to put emphasis on duration, not on results.

I’ve been running for ten hours.

For that reason, time structures like all day, long hours, for ages, since, recently, lately, etc. tend to accompany this verb tense.

3. When we are interested in the results of a recently finished action.

It’s been raining and the ground is wet.

4. While the present perfect simple gives the idea of completion, the present perfect continuous suggests that something is unfinished.

I’ve read Don Quixote. (I’ve already finished the book.)

I’ve been reading Don Quixote. (I haven’t finished it yet.)

 

NOTE: PRESENT PERFECT SIMPLE VS PRESENT PERFECT CONTINUOUS

The present perfect simple has a very similar use, so sometimes both tenses are interchangeable.

I’ve been living in Cádiz for ten years.

I’ve lived in Cádiz for ten years.

However, we prefer present perfect continuous for temporary actions and the present perfect simple tense for more permanent situations.

I’ve been working on this assignment all morning.

I’ve worked for this company all my life.

'

theory.heading: Prepositions
theory.subheading:
In

1. Inside the edges of something (room, car, pocket, box…).

In the kitchen, in a taxi, in a bag.

2. Contained within a continent, country, city or town.

In London, in Stratford-upon-Avon, in Europe, in Spain.

3. Months, years, centuries.

In January, in 2015, in the 21st century.

4. Certain parts of the day.

In the morning, in the afternoon, in the evening.

Note: At night is an exception here, as are parts of the day that refer to exact times, such as at noon, at dawn, at sunrise.

5. Large areas.

In the woods, in the park.

6. Expressions.

In the sky, in the newspaper, in a picture, in bed, in hospital, in prison, in three years’ time.

7. Certain vehicles.

In the car, in a taxi, in a helicopter, in a tank, in a rocket ship.

 

On

1. Surfaces.

The book is on the table.

2. Certain vehicles.

On the bus, on a plane, on a motorbike, on a ship.

3. Dates and days.

On Tuesday, on Monday morning, on the first of May, on September 4th.

4. Words or phrases that contain the word ‘day’.

On Christmas day, on holiday, on New Year’s Day.

5. Floors of a building.

He lives on the second floor.

6. Other phrases.

On a farm, on the beach, on the Internet, on a river, on a road, on TV.

 

At

1. Exact times.

Let’s meet at 5.00, meet me at 1pm.

2. Position at a particular point.

At the door, at the traffic lights.

3. Events.

At a party, at a concert, at the cinema.

4. Public places, when we refer to the activity not the building.

At the supermarket, at the library, at a shop.

5. Addresses.

I live at 56 Oxford Street.

6. With words or phrases that have the word ‘time’.

I’ll see you at lunch time.

7. Holidays and festivals.

At Christmas, at Easter.

8. Expressions.

At home, at work, at school, at university, at college. at church.

At the weekend, at the moment, at night.

'

theory.heading: Modal verbs
theory.subheading:
Here is a list of the main characteristics of modal verbs:

1. They do not change their form, so we don’t add ‘s’ or ‘-ing’ to them.

I can.
He can.
They can.

2. To make the negative form we add ‘not’ to the modal verb, not the main verb.

I cannot.
He mustn’t.
They shouldn’t.

3. In the interrogative form we change the order of the verb and the subject.

Can you?
Should I?
Would they?

4. They are usually followed by a bare infinitive (an infinitive without ‘to’).

I may come.
He could stay.
We wouldn’t go.
 

Exceptions

Ought to: is an exception because it requires a ‘to-infinitive verb’ but otherwise behaves like other modal verbs.

Have to: also needs a ‘to-infinitive verb’. In addition, the negative and interrogative forms of this verb require the auxiliary verb ‘do’, ‘does’ or ‘did’. In the third person singular it changes to ‘has’, and in the past it changes to ‘had’.

Be able to: also requires a ‘to-infinitive verb’ and it must change according to the tense needed, for instance: Is able to, was able to, will be able to, have been able to, etc.

There are two main types of modal verbs, simple modal verbs and perfect modal verbs.

Simple modal Verbs (and when to use them)
VerbsUseExample 
CanAbilities in the present.
Informal permission.
Offers and requests. 		He can swim.
Can I open the door?
Can I help you?
Can`t/CannotProhibition.
Certainty: When we are sure about something.		You cannot smoke in restaurants.
Jane is here. She can’t be in London.
CouldAbilities in the past.
Formal permission and requests.
Possibilities.		I could climb trees when I was a child.
Could you help me, sir?
I could meet you after work.
Couldn'tProhibition in the past.
Certainty in the past.		In the past, women couldn’t vote.
He couldn’t have done it, he was with me all day.
Be able toAbilities (all tenses).I have been able to swim since I was 5.
May/MightPossibility, probability: When we are not sure about something.
Very polite requests and asking/giving permission.		It may/might rain tomorrow.
May I come in? May I borrow your pencil?
MustObligation.
Certainty: Things you are sure about.		You must wear a uniform in this school.
He must be very tired, he has been working for 10 hours.
Mustn'tProhibition.You mustn’t talk in class.
Have to/Had toObligation (mainly external).You have to start work at 8.30 every morning.
Needn't/Don't have toLack of obligation.You don’t have to get up early on Sundays.
Should/Ought toAdvice. Opinion.You look very pale, you should see the doctor.
ShallSuggestions.
Offers.		Shall we go for a walk?
Shall I help you with those bags?

'

theory.heading: Perfect Modals
theory.subheading:
The structure of perfect modals goes as follows:

subject + modal verb + have + past participle

The main difference with simple modals is the use of ‘have’ + past participle after the modal verb, and the fact that they refer to the past most of the time.

Verbs Use Example
Could have + past participle Possibilities or abilities in the past (that didn’t happen). We could have won the match but they were the better team
Couldn't have + P.P Impossibility in the past. He couldn’t have arrived earlier. There was a traffic jam.
Shoud have + P.P Regret: You did or didn’t do something and you wish you had done the opposite. It was boiling hot during the summer. I should have installed an air conditioning unit.
May/Might have + P.P Possibility in the past that you are not sure about. What was that noise? It might have been a plane.
Needn't have + P.P Lack of obligation in the past. You needn’t have washed the dishes. I have a dish washer.
Must have + P.P Deduction or certainty in the past. Look at that gold ring. It must have cost a fortune!

theory.heading: Would
theory.subheading:
Would is a modal auxiliary verb. It is usually followed by a bare infinitive and is combined with the word ‘not’ to make negative forms.

I would buy a house if I could.
I would love to get know him.
I wouldn’t want to work there.
I wouldn’t do that.

Some of the most frequent uses of ‘would’ are:

1) In conditional sentences types 2 and 3:

If I were you, I would go to the doctor. (2)

If I won the lottery, I would buy a big house. (2)

If I had studied harder, I would have passed the exam. (3)

If I hadn’t missed the train, I wouldn’t have been late for work. (3)

2) Future in the past:

When we refer to a past action that had not yet happened at the time we are speaking about.

They rang to say that they would arrive soon.

He said he would make dinner.

3) Polite requests and offers:

Would you like a drink, madam?

Would you like a drink, madam?

4) Repeated actions and habits in the past, not including past states. In the case of past states, ‘used to’ is the correct option. However, ‘used to’ can also be used to refer to habits that we had in the past but don’t have any more:

When I was a child, I would play hide and seek with my friends.

We would see him from time to time and he was always happy to see us.

I used to go to the gym four times a week but lately I don’t have the time.

When I was a child, I used to be very shy.

'

theory.title: Question Tags
theory.subheading:
A question tag is a short question at the end of a statement. We use these questions when we are asking for confirmation.

If the statement is affirmative, the question tag is negative. Similarly, if the statement is negative, the question tag is affirmative.

You live in London, don’t you?

Alex isn’t a teacher, is he?

You must use auxiliary verbs with question tags, just as we do in interrogative sentences. There are two special cases:

When ‘to be’ is the main verb, we can use <>‘to be’ in the question tag.

The verb ‘to be’
I am late, aren’t I?

When making a suggestion, we can use ‘shall’ in the question tag

Suggestions
Let’s go home, shall we?

The intonation is essential. If the tone goes up on the question tag it means you don’t know for sure and need confirmation. If, on the other hand, the tone goes down, you are just keeping the conversation going.

To confirm information
Ana doesn’t work here, does she? (up)
To keep the conversation going
Ana speaks English, doesn’t she? (down)

theory.heading: Relative Clauses
theory.subheading:
We use relative pronouns to introduce relative clauses, which give us more information about people and things. Take a look at this list of relative pronouns and the things that we use them to talk about:

Who: is used to talk about people.
Which: is used to talk about things.
That: can be used to talk about both people and things.
When: is used for time references.
Where: is used for place references.
Whose: is not a pronoun but a possessive determiner, so it must be followed by a noun.

Whom

Whom is the formal form of ‘who’. It can be used as the object of the clause. We don’t use it very often and it sounds quite old fashioned to most native speakers, although it’s slightly more commonly used with writing.

When we do use ‘whom’, we tend to use it with a preposition. When writing formally, we usually place the preposition before ‘whom’, rather than after it:

John is the person for whom I have been working all these years.

However, usually we would just say ‘John is the person (who) I have been working for all these years’.

'

The most well known clause containing the word whom is used when addressing a letter to an unknown recipient. Instead of writing ‘Dear Sir/Madam’, we may choose to keep the letter as impersonal as possible by writing ‘To whom it may concern’. This is not a particularly friendly way to begin a letter so you should never use it when writing to people that you already know personally.

'

Defining and Non-defining Clauses

 

We use defining relative clauses to give essential information, for example:

This is the boy who broke the window.

In this case, we need this extra information to define who exactly we are talking about.

We use non-defining relative clauses to add information that is not necessary. They go between commas and are very useful. For example:

London, which is the capital of England, is among the oldest of the world's great cities.

In order for this type of clause to be correct, we must be able to remove it from the sentence entirely and leave behind a sentence that works without any changes. So, if we were to remove the non-defining relative clause from the example above, we would have:

London is among the oldest of the world’s great cities.

As the sentence still makes sense and doesn’t require any changes, we know that we have used this type of clause correctly.

 

Note: We never use ‘that’ in non-defining relative clauses.

 

Omission of the Relative Pronoun

There are certain relative pronouns, who, which, that and when, that can sometimes be omitted from a clause entirely. They are commonly removed from the sentence in spoken and informal English. When the pronoun is the object of the relative clause, the pronoun ‘that’ can be left out.

That’s the man (who) I lent the book to.

That’s the pen (which) I lent you last week.

She’s the one (that) I asked on a date.

'

'

theory.subheading:
The article 'the' and zero article

 

When to use the definite article ‘the’ in English can be very confusing. Here are some tips:
We use ‘the’:

1. When we are talking about something that the interlocutor already knows about.

I know that you prefer the sports car but I think we should buy the cheaper one.

I’m meeting the girl that I introduced you to last week.

When I walked by, I noticed you had left your keys in the front door.

2. When that thing or person is unique and there is only one of them.

The Sun

The Earth

The King of Spain

3. When we have already talked about it previously.

I saw a dog on the street today. The dog was very dirty and looked hungry.

I walked into a sweet shop. The shop was full of children eager to eat sweeties.

If you met an alien and it offered to give you a tour of the galaxy on its spaceship, would you go with the alien or not?

4. Whenever the noun it goes together with is very limited by the use of prepositional phrases, adjectives, relative clauses or similar specifications.

The girl that you are dating is very nice.

I don’t like the blue scarf but the red one.

5. With names for the following types of thing: Musical instruments, newspapers, hotels, restaurants, mountain ranges, groups of islands, rivers, seas and oceans.

I travelled to the Alps.

He lives near the river Tyne.

The Guardian is one of the most popular newspapers in Britain.

I play the guitar.

I would love to stay at The Ritz at least once in my life.

'

You mustn’t use ‘the’:

1. When talking about things or people in general, usually in the plural or with singular uncountable nouns.

People believe everything they read in the news.

Computers are so much faster than they used to be.

Salt and sugar are really bad for your health, if you eat too much of them.

Money is all that matters to some people.

2. With names for the following types of thing: Languages, most countries and continents, festivals, cities, lakes and individual mountains (as opposed to mountain ranges).

I speak Chinese.

He lives in Switzerland..

I love Christmas.

Note: Countries that are made up of a group of islands sometimes use the definite article, for example, we say the Philippines but not the Indonesia.

Also, we tend to place the definite article before countries that contain words like united, republic or federation but not if we use the common, alternative names for these countries. Here are a few examples:

America or the United States of America.

Russia or the Russian Federation.

China or the People’s Republic of China.

3. With ‘home’, ‘prison’, ‘hospital’, ‘school’ and ‘university’ when they are used for their usual purpose.

He went to prison for robbery.

I am at school doing my class work.

I have had a long day of study at university.

Note: When referencing which particular university we study/studied at (focusing on making a distinction from other universities) then we use the definite article if we put the place name after ‘University of’, but not if we put the place name before ‘University’. For example:

She studied at the University of York.

She studied at York University.

theory.heading: Conditional Sentences
theory.subheading:
Conditional sentences are used to express that the action in the main clause, the one without if, can only take place if a certain condition, described in the if clause, is fulfilled. For example:

If you heat water, it boils.
I will help you, if you give me some money.
If he relaxed more, he would be happier.
You would have passed the exam, if you had worked harder.

 

Conditional type 0

If clause: Present simple
Main clause: Present simple

This type of conditional refers to our habits, general truths or scientific facts.

If it rains, the ground gets wet.
If I get tired, I go to bed early.
If you pour oil on water, it floats.

In zero conditional sentences, ‘if’ can be replaced with ‘when’ without any change in meaning.

When it rains, the ground gets wet.

 

Conditional type 1

If clause: Present simple
Main clause: Future will

The first conditional expresses possibility. We use it when we consider that it is possible that the condition will be fulfilled, although it is not an absolute certainty.

If I study hard, I will pass the exam. He’’ll go on a picnic, if it doesn’t rain tomorrow. If they finish work on time, they’ll meet us here.

NOTE: ‘Can’, ‘may’ and imperative can be part of the main clause instead of ‘will’.

We can spend the day out if it doesn’t rain heavily.
You may see him at the party tonight, if he attends.
If he gets angry, tell him the truth.

 

Conditional type 2

If clause: Past simple
Main clause: Would + infinitive

The second conditional refers to improbable situations. We can use it for things that are impossible in the present or unlikely to happen in the future.

If I won the lottery, I would give some money to you.
She would make a lot more money, if she changed her profession.
If he listened to others more, maybe he would learn something.

One particularly common application of the second conditional is used when we want to put ourselves in another person’s position, or put them in our position. When we do this, we tend to use the subjunctive ‘were’, regardless of the pronoun. We often do this when giving or asking for advice.

If I were you, I’d apply for the job.
What would you do, if you were me?

In a similar way to conditional type 1, modals such as ‘might’ and ‘could’ can replace ‘would’.

If I went to Paris, I could see the Eiffel Tower.
She might be a great athlete, if she trained harder.

 

Conditional type 3

If clause: Past perfect
Main clause: Would have + past part.

The third conditional refers to things that didn’t happen in the past and their imaginary results. Of course, anything that is speculated on in the third conditional is considered to be impossible, because it is already too late to change it.

If he had been born two minutes earlier, he would have been the older twin.
I wouldn’t have agreed to meet him, if I had known he was so rude.
If they had left on time, they would have been here by now.

In the same way as conditional type 2, modals such as ‘might’ and ‘could’ can replace ‘would’.

If he had studied harder, he might have passed the exam.
I could have been a contender, if I had done my best.

 

Note: The past participle of the verb ‘to have’ is ‘had’, so be aware that when using it in the if clause, you may need to use ‘had’ twice in a row. This looks strange but, don’t worry, it’s completely grammatically accurate. When we do this though, we usually contract the first ‘had’ into a -’d. For example:

If he had had a better childhood, he would have been a nicer person.
If I’d had more training, I could have won the race.
If she’d had more friends, things might have been easier.

Other expressions (instead of 'if')

Conditional clauses can also begin with: Unless, provided (that), providing, as long as, so long as and on conditiont that.
These cases tend to stick to conditionals type zero and one.

Unless you have a visa, you won’t be allowed to travel to the USA.
You may come over tonight, provided you bring a bottle of wine.
So long as the flag is raised, the camp can be seen in the distance.

 

Inverted Conditionals

In very formal situations, conditionals types 1, 2 and 3 have an alternative in which the verbs change the logical order of the clauses. These are called inverted conditionals.

 

Inversion type 1

Should + subject + infinitive verb

Should I have the time, I will drop by and we’ll have a coffee. (= If I have the time…)
Should you require any further information, contact us. (= If you require any further information...)

 

Inversion type 2

Were + subject + to infinitive

Were the two Prime Ministers to meet up, some issues would be fixed. (= If the Prime Ministers met up…)
Were he older, his recovery would be much quicker. (= If he were older...)

 

Inversion type 3

Had + subject + past participle

Had Harold won the Battle of Hastings, Britain would have been a very different country now. (= If Harold had won the Battle of Hastings...)
Had the Normans not won the battle, what would have happened? (= If the Norman’s hadn’t won the battle...)

exercise.description: Select the appropriate headings for each paragraph from the phrases or words given bellow.
exercise.label: Reading Part 4

answers: A) The neglect of a historical treasure
B) Ancient Greece all over the globe
C) The lesser of two evils?
D) The end of Greek civilization
questions.title: 1. Regarded by many as the best museum in the world, the British Museum has also received a good deal of criticism. In fact, it has been criticised for quite some time over one main issue, namely the display of artefacts and works of art that did not originally belong to the UK.{v} While the British Museum currently houses around eight million pieces, some of its more famous works, such as the Benin Bronzes, the Rosetta Stone and The Elgin Marbles, are important parts of the history of other nations. Many people believe that these pieces should be returned to their countries of origin, challenging the morality of a national institution possessing artefacts that were taken from foreign civilizations, but how did they end up in Britain in the first place?

answers: A) The neglect of a historical treasure
B) Ancient Greece all over the globe
C) The lesser of two evils?
D) The end of Greek civilization
questions.title: 2. The British Museum’s most famous controversy surrounds the Elgin Marbles.{v} The Elgin Marbles have been in the possession of Great Britain since 1801, when Lord Elgin, a British nobleman, stripped the Parthenon of a great number of its sculptures. How was this possible?{v} Well, since the 15th century, Greece had been ruled by the Ottoman Empire. This powerful empire had not only neglected the care of this great work by the ancient Athenian, Phidias, but they had also made use of the historical monument to store artillery.

answers: A) The neglect of a historical treasure
B) Ancient Greece all over the globe
C) The lesser of two evils?
D) The end of Greek civilization
questions.title: 3. When we take the use of the Parthenon as an ammunitions depot into account, it is no wonder that, during one of the Ottoman Empire’s wars, the Venetians destroyed part of the Parthenon and many of its sculptures were smashed to pieces. This event paved the way for the looting of the site. Its ruined decor encouraged almost anyone to take pieces home, since no true ownership of the site was established. This, of course, became a call to antique collectors, who were always on the lookout for treasures such as these. Moreover, these collectors claimed that their actions were not illegal, since the Ottoman Empire itself was the party responsible for damaging the Parthenon. Their reasons, they said, were simply to protect these historical artefacts from total destruction.

answers: A) The neglect of a historical treasure
B) Ancient Greece all over the globe
C) The lesser of two evils?
D) The end of Greek civilization
questions.title: 4. In addition to this rare opportunity, Lord Elgin was, in fact, an ambassador to the Ottoman Empire, and it is said he was given permission to remove such items in return for the services he had offered the Sultan when he was fighting against Napoleon. An alternative theory states that what he really did was to use his privileged position as an ambassador to persuade the sultan to give him and his men the authority to remove pieces of the ancient temple.

answers: A) The neglect of a historical treasure
B) Ancient Greece all over the globe
C) The lesser of two evils?
D) The end of Greek civilization
questions.title: 5. Whether they were actually given free rein to remove these artefacts from the Parthenon or not, is still a mystery. However, the end result is that the British Museum is now home to numerous capitals, metopes and columns that used to decorate the iconic Greek temple. Equally, it must not be forgotten that there are fragments from the Parthenon held in many other cities around the world too, including St Petersburg, Istanbul, Paris and New York.

answers: A) The neglect of a historical treasure
B) Ancient Greece all over the globe
C) The lesser of two evils?
D) The end of Greek civilization
questions.title: 6. On the surface, the repatriation of all artefacts to their countries of origin seems to be the right thing to do. However, one important factor we should consider is whether or not these historical pieces would be better looked after elsewhere. Firstly, far fewer people would be able to study these works of art if they were scattered across the world in a variety of countries and storage conditions. Secondly, unstable countries, like Uganda for instance, seem incredibly unlikely to safely maintain valuable artefacts that reflect their past. Great Britain, on the other hand, is historically a very stable country that has proven its ability to protect these priceless works of human civilization from harm for hundreds of years.

answers: A) The neglect of a historical treasure
B) Ancient Greece all over the globe
C) The lesser of two evils?
D) The end of Greek civilization
questions.title: 7. All things considered, intuitively most of us probably feel that objects that have been stolen or taken by foreigners should be rightly returned to their country of origin, but the key question remains, would the remedy be worse than the disease?

theory.heading: Passive Voice
theory.subheading:
The passive voice is used when we want to emphasise the object, the person or thing that experiences the action, instead of the subject, the person or thing performing the action. So, when we change a sentence from active to passive, the object becomes the subject of the passive sentence. We cannot change sentences that do not contain an object into passive voice.

I bought some pears. (active)
Some pears were bought. (passive)
I went to the park yesterday. (active but with no object, so cannot be passive)

Changes must be made to the verb as well. There are three steps:

1. Determine what verb tense is being used in the active sentence.
2. Add the verb ‘be’, in the tense determined in part 1, to the passive sentence.
3. Add the past participle of the main verb (in the active sentence) to the passive sentence.

Let’s look at an example:

I am writing a letter. (active)

We can determine that the tense being used is present continuous, so we use the present continuous of the verb ‘be’, which is ‘is being’. Then we add the past participle of ‘writing’, which is ‘written’.

A letter is being written. (passive)

The passive can be used with all tenses. We just need to change the form of ‘be’.

You must do your homework. (active)
Your homework must be done. (passive)

He hasn’t finished his work. (active)
His work hasn’t been finished. (passive)

When we are interested in ‘who’ performed the action, we may add an agent complement using the preposition ‘by’.

My sister will send the letter. (active)
The letter will be sent by my sister. (passive)

When the doer or agent of the action is an instrument, we use ‘with’.

The door was smashed open with a hammer.

However, most of the time, we do not need to include these prepositional phrases because either we don’t know/care about the agent, or because it is obvious.


This castle was built in 1246. (but we don’t know/care who built it)

The car has been repaired. (a mechanic did it, obviously)
Taxes will be increased by November. (by the government, obviously)

Passive with two objects:

When there are two objects in an active sentence, this makes two passive sentences possible. The indirect object is usually a person and the direct one an object.

I sent your sister a letter. (‘sister’ and ‘letter’ are both objects)

This means we can make the sentence passive in one of two ways:

Your sister was sent a letter.
Or
A letter was sent to your sister.

As you can see from this last example, when a direct object is followed by an indirect one, we put ‘to’ in front of the indirect object.

'

Verb changes from active to passive

TenseActivePassive
Simple Presentcleanam/are/is cleaned
Present Continuousam/are/is cleaningam/are/is being cleaned
Past Continuouswas/were cleaningwas/were being cleaned
Present Perfecthave/has cleanedhave/has been cleaned
Past Perfecthad cleanedhad been cleaned
Simple Future
will		will cleanwill be cleaned
Simple Future
be going to		am/are/is going to cleanam/are/is going to be cleaned
Future Perfect
will		will have cleanedwill have been cleaned
Wouldwould cleanwould be cleaned
Can (+modals)can cleancan be cleaned

 

Impersonal Passive

We use impersonal passive with verbs like: Say, think, suggest, believe, report, expect, consider, etc.

There are two patterns:

It + passive + that clause
Subject + passive + to infinitive

Active sentence: People say that children are noisy.
Passive sentence 1: It is said that children are noisy.
Passive sentence 2: Children are said to be noisy.

 

Form Passive to Active

Sometimes you may be asked to turn a passive voice sentence into an active one. So, you must think about it the other way round. First, the subject becomes the object. Then the tense of the verb ‘be’ becomes the tense of the main verb. Finally, you need to make the subject of the action explicit.

The flowers were bought yesterday. (passive)
The girl bought the flowers yesterday. (active)

The flat will be cleaned by the owner. (passive)
The owner will clean the flat. (active)

theory.heading: Reported Speech
theory.subheading:
We use reported speech when we want to tell someone what somebody said at an earlier time, but without using their exact words. We can report the speech of anyone, including ourselves and the person we are talking to. In order to do so, the tenses of the verbs, most pronouns, some adverbs and the time expressions have to be changed:

He said that he had bought a new bike for his son the day before.

This is the opposite of direct speech, where we make use of quotation marks to quote someone’s exact words:

He said, ‘I bought a bike for my son yesterday’.

Here's a table of some possible conversions of adverbs, determiners and time expressions.

thisthat
thesethose
herethere
nowthen/at that time
todaythat day
yestedaythe day before
last nightthe night before, the previous night
last weekthe week before, the previous week
tomorrowthe next day, the following day

There are several types of sentence that we can turn into reported speech: Statements, questions, commands and suggestions.
As a result, there are at least four ways of reporting them.

 

Statements

The reporting verb in statements is often ‘said’ but it can also be ‘told’, or other verbs like ‘stated’, or ‘claimed’, depending on the situation. ‘That’ can be added but it is optional and doesn’t affect the meaning:

He said that he was happy.
Or
He said he was happy.

The tenses of the verbs usually go back in time. They change in the following way:

presentpast
pastpast perfect
willwould
cancould
maymight
mustmust/had to

Each tense will change but their aspect will remain the same. For example, present continuous changes to past continuous, and past simple changes to past perfect simple.

However, there are certain verb tenses that do not change. These are past perfect, would, should, ought to and might.

'I have lost my keys today’, he said.
He said that he had lost his keys that day.

Questions

When we report a question we usually use the verb ‘asked’, although others can also be correct, such as ‘wanted to say’, ‘wondered’ and ‘wanted to know’.

If the question is a yes/no question, we must use either ‘if’ or ‘whether’. The changes to verbs, pronouns and others must be applied too:

‘Did you study for the test last Sunday?’, said Ann.
Ann asked if I had studied for the test the previous Sunday.

If it is a question with an interrogative word, the same interrogative word remains:

‘Where are you going tomorrow?’, he asked her.
He asked her where she was going the following day.

 

Commands

When we report a command or imperative we use ‘told’, ‘asked’ or ‘ordered’ + the person we are talking to + ‘to/not to’ + infinitive.

In this case, the tense of the verb does not go back in time. However, pronouns and adverbs must be changed when necessary:

Stand up’, said the teacher.
The teacher told us to stand up.

‘Don’t use your mobile phones’, said the teacher.
The teacher told us not to use our mobile phones.

 

Suggestions

Suggestions in English are expressed by using phrases like:

Let’s + infinitive, shall we + infinitive, how/what about + -ing, why not+ infinitive, why don’t you + infinitive, and we could+ infinitive.

When reporting them we must use one of the following three specific patterns:

1) that clause + should
2) that clause + subjunctive (infinitive form)
3) -ing form

For example:

‘Let’s give my sister a call’, she said.

1) She suggested that we should give her sister a call.

2) She suggested that we give her sister a call.

3) She suggested giving her sister a call.
